subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) ZIP extension helper. extractFileTo() is the per-entry worker that backs ZipArchive::extractTo / the zip_* userland API: given an open libzip archive handle, an entry name, and a destination-path accumulator, it creates any parent directories and writes the decompressed contents of one archive member to the local filesystem.
provenance: Identified by the HHVM-specific macros HHVM_FN(is_dir)/HHVM_FN(mkdir) (built-in dispatch wrappers) and FAIL_IF_INVALID_PTR (an HHVM extension error-return macro), combined with the libzip C API (zip*, struct zip_stat, zip_stat, zip_fopen_index, zip_fread, zip_fclose). The signature and std::string usage match hphp/runtime/ext/zip/ext_zip.cpp. The banner comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2213_vuln */' and the file name (train_0322_cwe22.cpp) are corpus metadata, not source; the review is on the merits of the code.
trust_boundary: The libzip archive is attacker-controlled: a user (or remote client) supplies a .zip file that Hack/PHP code opens and extracts. The entry name passed in as `file` is read verbatim from the archive's central directory and is fully hostile — it may contain '../' traversal sequences, a leading '/', embedded NUL, or point at a symlink path. `to` is the intended output directory prefix chosen by the application, but it is combined with the untrusted `file` with no canonicalization or containment check, so the effective write path is attacker-influenced. `buf`/`len` are a caller-owned scratch buffer for decompressed bytes.

what_the_code_does.code:
static bool extractFileTo(zip* zip, const std::string &file, std::string& to,
                          char* buf, size_t len) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the extraction worker. `zip` is an open libzip archive handle. `file` is the name of the entry to extract, taken from the archive metadata (attacker-controlled). `to` is passed by non-const reference and serves as a mutable path accumulator: on entry it holds the destination directory prefix, and the function appends onto it in place. `buf` with size `len` is a reusable decompression buffer supplied by the caller. The function returns bool for success/failure. Because `to` is a reference that the function mutates (line 16), its state is observably changed for the caller.

Finds the last '/' in the entry name to split it into a directory portion and a leaf name. `rfind` returns npos when the entry has no slash (a top-level file). If a slash exists, the block that follows is responsible for creating the entry's parent directory tree before the file itself is written. Note that no inspection of the path components is performed here — '..' and leading '/' are treated as ordinary path text.

Builds the parent-directory path by concatenating the destination prefix `to` with the directory portion of the entry name (everything before the last slash). It then checks whether that directory already exists via is_dir(); if not, it calls mkdir() with mode 0777 and the recursive flag = true, so the entire missing path is created. If the directory neither exists nor can be created, the function fails. Crucially, `path` is derived directly from the untrusted `file` with no removal or rejection of '../' segments, and the directory is created world-readable/writable/executable (0777).

Handles pure directory entries: if the last '/' is the final character of the entry name, the entry represents a directory (no file body), so after ensuring the directory exists the function returns success without opening or writing any file. This is the normal ZIP convention where directory members end in '/'.

Appends the FULL entry name (including any directory portion) onto the destination accumulator `to`, forming the final on-disk path for the output file. Because `to` is a reference, this permanently mutates the caller's string. As with line 6, the entry name is concatenated verbatim: no canonicalization, no check that the result stays under the intended base directory.

Queries libzip for metadata about the named entry, populating `zipStat`. The entry is looked up by name (file.c_str()), and the resulting zipStat.index is later used to open the member. A non-zero return means the entry could not be stat'd, so the function bails. .c_str() is used inline as a call argument, so there is no lifetime concern with the returned pointer.

Opens the archive member by its index (obtained from the stat above) for reading, returning a zip_file* stream. FAIL_IF_INVALID_PTR is an HHVM macro that returns/throws an error if zipFile is null (e.g. corrupt or encrypted entry), so subsequent code can assume a valid handle.

Opens the destination path (the mutated `to`) for binary writing, truncating/creating it. On failure it closes the archive stream to avoid a leak and returns false. This fopen is the sink where the attacker-controlled path is actually acted upon: whatever path traversal or symlink target is embedded in the entry name determines which file on disk is created or overwritten here.

Streams the decompressed contents: each iteration reads up to `len` bytes from the archive member into `buf` (n = bytes read; 0 = EOF, negative = error) and writes exactly n bytes to the output file. If a read error occurs (n < 0) or fwrite writes fewer than n elements (short write / I/O error), it closes both handles, deletes the partially written output via remove(), and fails. zip_fread returns a signed 64-bit count, and the n < 0 test short-circuits before the fwrite comparison, so the size_t-vs-signed comparison in fwrite(...) != n only runs when n is positive.

On clean EOF, closes the archive member stream, then flushes and closes the output file, checking fclose's return so that buffered-write errors surfacing at close time are reported as failure. Returns true on full success. Note that unlike the loop's error path, this failure path does not remove() the (now possibly corrupt) output file, and does not close nothing further — a minor inconsistency, not a security bug.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: PATH TRAVERSAL / ZIP SLIP (CWE-22). The final output path is `to` + the untrusted entry name `file`, with zero containment enforcement. An attacker who controls the archive can name an entry '../../../../etc/cron.d/pwn', '../../.ssh/authorized_keys', or (since nothing strips a leading slash) a path that, after append, escapes the extraction root. fopen(to.c_str(), "wb") at line 25 then creates or truncates that arbitrary file with the archive-supplied contents — a write-anything-anywhere primitive leading to remote code execution or privilege escalation depending on where HHVM runs. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: canonicalize the joined path (resolve '.'/'..'/symlinks) and reject the entry unless the real path is strictly inside the intended base directory, before any mkdir/fopen. Rejecting entries containing '..' components or a leading '/' is the standard fix.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
auto path = to + file.substr(0, sep);
    if (!HHVM_FN(is_dir)(path) && !HHVM_FN(mkdir)(path, 0777, true)) {
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: TRAVERSAL VIA DIRECTORY CREATION (CWE-22). The same lack of sanitization applies to the parent-directory path, and here it is worse because mkdir is recursive: an entry named '../../../../var/www/evil/x' causes the whole '../../../../var/www/evil' chain to be created outside the extraction root before the file is even written. So the traversal is exploitable at the directory-creation stage independently of the file write. The implementation must validate the directory portion under the same containment rule as the leaf, before calling mkdir.

SYMLINK FOLLOWING (CWE-59). fopen(..., "wb") follows symbolic links. Combined with the traversal above (or with a symlink an attacker planted, e.g. via an earlier archive entry that is itself a symlink extracted into the tree), a crafted archive can point the output at a symlink whose target is a sensitive file, causing the write to land on the link target rather than inside the extraction directory. This is the classic archive symlink-escape. The safe pattern is to open with O_NOFOLLOW / O_EXCL semantics and to refuse to extract entries onto existing symlinks, and to never extract archive members that are themselves symlinks without validation.

OVERLY PERMISSIVE DIRECTORY MODE (CWE-732 / CWE-276). Directories are created with mode 0777, which (subject to umask) can leave them world-writable. Any local user can then drop or replace files inside the extracted tree, enabling tampering or, if the extracted content is later executed/served, code execution. The invariant is to create extraction directories with a restrictive mode (e.g. 0755 or tighter) rather than 0777. Lower severity than the traversal, but a real hardening defect on the same line.

MUTATING SHARED STATE VIA REFERENCE (correctness / robustness). `to` is taken by non-const reference and is permanently extended by both the mkdir path construction (via a copy at line 6, which is fine) and the in-place append at line 16. If the caller extracts multiple entries by reusing the same `to` accumulator in a loop, each subsequent entry's path is built on top of the previous entry's full path unless the caller resets `to` every iteration — producing wrong output paths or extraction failures. This is not itself a memory-safety bug, but it makes the containment story harder to reason about and can interact with the traversal (an unexpected base). Confirm the caller resets `to` per entry and passes a trusted, already-canonical base.

summary: This is a textbook Zip Slip: extractFileTo() joins an attacker-controlled archive entry name onto a destination prefix and feeds the result straight to mkdir (recursive, mode 0777) and fopen with no canonicalization or containment check. A crafted .zip with '../' or absolute-path entries yields arbitrary directory creation and arbitrary file write/overwrite (CWE-22), amplified by symlink following at the fopen sink and world-writable directory permissions. Critical severity.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: extractFileTo(zip*, const std::string& file, std::string& to, char* buf, size_t len) signature (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: `file` is untrusted archive data and `to` is a mutable reference accumulator combined with it without validation — the root of the traversal contract.
Just splits leaf from directory; no security decision is made here, and it does not reject or normalize '..'.
Untrusted directory portion concatenated onto the base with no containment check; feeds recursive mkdir.
Recursive directory creation on the traversal path AND world-permissive 0777 mode — two distinct defects (CWE-22 and CWE-732).
Correctly returns early for trailing-slash directory members; carries no unsafe contract.
Forms the final output path from the untrusted full entry name with no canonicalization; the core Zip Slip sink.
.c_str() used inline as a call argument (no dangling-pointer lifetime issue); lookup by name is not a filesystem write.
Null-checked before use; no unsafe dereference or size arithmetic.
Write sink for the traversed path and a symlink-following primitive (CWE-59).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: zip_fread/fwrite copy loop and n<0 guard (lines 31-39)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Read is bounded by caller-supplied `len`; n<0 short-circuits before the signed/unsigned fwrite comparison, so bytes written == bytes read is well-defined; no overflow.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: fwrite(buf, sizeof(char), n, outFile) != n comparison (line 33)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Signed/unsigned comparison is only reached when n>0, so it is well-defined; not a defect.
Handles are freed on all paths; the only nit is not remove()-ing the file on close failure, which is not a security issue.

findings.title: Zip Slip: arbitrary file write via unsanitized archive entry name (path traversal)
findings.bug_class: Path traversal / directory traversal (Zip Slip) enabling arbitrary file write
findings.invariant: Before any mkdir or fopen, the joined path (base `to` + entry `file`) must be canonicalized (resolving '.', '..', and symlinks) and rejected unless the resulting real path is strictly contained within the intended extraction base directory; leading '/' and any '..' component must be refused.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker supplies a .zip whose entry name is e.g. '../../../../etc/cron.d/pwn' or '../../.ssh/authorized_keys'. extractFileTo appends it to `to` and fopen("wb") creates/truncates that file outside the extraction root with attacker-chosen contents, giving arbitrary file write -> remote code execution or privilege escalation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether the caller or this function canonicalizes and containment-checks the joined path before extraction. In the shown code there is no such check, so confirm there is no compensating guard upstream in ZipArchive::extractTo.

findings.title: Recursive directory creation on the traversal path (mkdir escapes extraction root)
findings.anchor: auto path = to + file.substr(0, sep); ... HHVM_FN(mkdir)(path, 0777, true)
findings.code:
auto path = to + file.substr(0, sep);
    if (!HHVM_FN(is_dir)(path) && !HHVM_FN(mkdir)(path, 0777, true)) {
findings.bug_class: Path traversal via recursive directory creation
findings.invariant: The directory portion of the entry must be validated under the same containment rule as the file, before the recursive mkdir is issued.
findings.failure_mode: An entry named '../../../../var/tmp/attacker/x' makes mkdir(recursive=true) create the entire '../../../../var/tmp/attacker' chain outside the extraction root, independent of the subsequent file write. This both escapes the sandbox and can be used to stage directories for later symlink/file-planting attacks.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that file.substr(0, sep) is validated for '..'/absolute components before mkdir; in the shown code it is not.

findings.title: Output file opened with symlink-following (link-based extraction escape)
findings.invariant: The write must not traverse a symbolic link out of the extraction directory: open with O_NOFOLLOW on the final component (and validate each component), and refuse to extract entries that are themselves symlinks pointing outside the root.
findings.failure_mode: fopen(..., "wb") follows symlinks. If a symlink exists at the target path (planted by an earlier archive entry or already on disk), the archive contents are written to the link's target — a sensitive file outside the extraction directory — bypassing even a purely lexical path check.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether extraction refuses symlink entries and opens output with O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL semantics; the shown fopen does neither.

findings.title: Extraction directories created world-writable (mode 0777)
findings.bug_class: Insecure default permissions on created directories
findings.invariant: Directories created during extraction should use a restrictive mode (e.g. 0755 or tighter), not 0777, so that other local users cannot tamper with extracted content.
findings.failure_mode: Subject to umask, the created directory is world-writable. A local attacker can replace or add files under the extracted tree; if that content is later executed or served, this becomes tampering or code execution.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended permission policy; 0777 is almost certainly too broad for extraction output.

findings.title: Destination path accumulator mutated through a shared reference
findings.bug_class: State corruption via in-place mutation of a caller reference
findings.invariant: The caller must pass a fresh, trusted, canonical base directory for `to` on every entry (or the function should build a local copy) so that per-entry paths do not accumulate.
findings.failure_mode: Because `to` is mutated in place at line 16, a caller that reuses the same accumulator across archive entries will build each path on top of the previous full path, producing wrong output locations or failures — and complicates any containment reasoning about the base.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect the caller loop in ext_zip.cpp to confirm `to` is reset per entry and is a trusted base.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether the joined path (to + file) is canonicalized and containment-checked against the extraction root before mkdir/fopen — the shown code has no such guard (Zip Slip).
Verify entry names are rejected when they contain '..' path components or a leading '/' (absolute path escape).
Check that output is opened with O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL and that symlink archive entries are refused, to prevent link-target overwrites.
Confirm the directory creation mode (0777) is intentional; tighten to 0755 or apply a stricter umask.
Inspect the caller of extractFileTo to ensure `to` is reset to the trusted base for every entry and that the base itself is validated.
Verify that ZipArchive::extractTo enforces any documented safe-extraction behavior (e.g. PHP-style path stripping) upstream of this helper.
